title: What the Q4 Superannuation Due Date Adds to Your July Cash Picture
linkedin:
One week from 30 June, most business owners are watching three things: the debtors ledger, the year-end spending window, and what the bank balance will look like before July starts.

There is a fourth variable that belongs on the same list.

The superannuation guarantee for April, May, and June is due on or before 28 July. It is not a June 30 obligation. For most businesses, it has not left the account yet. But it is sitting there as a fixed, calculable amount owed from the quarter that just closed.

For a business with twenty staff earning in a typical service-business range, the Q4 super obligation is easily $30,000 to $50,000 or more depending on earnings. It lands in week three or four of July, in the same window as the first payroll at the new award rates and the tail of June receivables still clearing through the account.

When these three items arrive together, the July bank balance rarely looks as comfortable as the June 30 balance suggested it would.

The calculation is not complicated. Pull the Q4 super liability from your payroll platform, add it to your July payroll forecast at the new rates, and compare the total against what you expect to have in the account that week. Most businesses that run this exercise find July's tightest point falls around 24 to 28 July, not the 1st.

That knowledge, three weeks in advance, is worth considerably more than the same knowledge arriving with the bank statement.

The new post on the ProfitPulse blog walks through the Q4 super timing, why July is typically the most cash-intensive month of the year, and the planning work available before June 30.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog. Link in the first comment.

Super guarantee for Q4 (April to June) is due 28 July, not 30 June. Most businesses know this. Fewer have actually added that specific outflow to their July cash plan alongside the new payroll rates and the tail of June receivables still clearing.

The question worth asking before the year closes: what does your bank balance look like in the week of 28 July, after all three of those items have run through?

New post on the ProfitPulse blog covers why 28 July deserves as much planning attention as 30 June, and the calculation worth running before the financial year closes.

google:
With one week until 30 June, business owners across Queensland and the East Coast are focused on their year-end cash position. One item that often sits outside the June 30 picture is the superannuation guarantee for the April to June quarter, which is due to be received by employees' funds on or before 28 July.

For most businesses, this is one of the largest single cash outflows in any given July, and it arrives in the same period as the first payroll at the new award rates and the final weeks of June receivables clearing through the account. The three items together mean the week of 24 to 28 July is often the tightest of the year, even when the June 30 bank balance looked comfortable.

Building a forward cash view this week, before the year closes, maps this obligation alongside the rest of July so the timing is visible in advance rather than in the bank statement.

ProfitPulse works with owner-led businesses across Queensland and the East Coast to build this kind of financial clarity before obligations arrive. Read the full piece on the blog.
